items.title: Image classifier over three animal classes
items.scenario: A CNN outputs logits z = [3.2, 0.5, −1.0] for cat, dog, bird. Softmax gives π ≈ [0.93, 0.06, 0.01]. The model predicts cat with high confidence while still assigning small probability to dog and bird.
items.explanation: Softmax converts unbounded scores into a probability simplex; the largest logit dominates but never assigns exactly 1.0 to any class unless one logit is vastly larger than the rest.

items.title: Language model vocabulary distribution
items.scenario: At the next-token position, a transformer emits 50,000 logits. Softmax produces a distribution over the entire vocabulary; the true next token 'Paris' might have π = 0.04 while the argmax token 'the' has π = 0.12.
items.explanation: Softmax at scale is the same formula: each token gets a positive probability. Training minimizes −log π on the true token — cross-entropy over the vocabulary.

items.title: Overflow without log-sum-exp
items.scenario: Logits z = [1000, 999, 998] in float32. Naive exp(z) overflows to inf, producing NaN probabilities. Subtracting m = 1000 gives exp([0, −1, −2]) = [1, 0.368, 0.135], sum ≈ 1.50, and stable π ≈ [0.67, 0.25, 0.09].
items.explanation: Shift-invariance of softmax makes the max-subtraction trick exact, not an approximation — it is the standard way to compute softmax and log-softmax safely.

items.title: Temperature flattening for calibration
items.scenario: A model outputs overconfident logits z = [10, 1, 1]. At T = 1, π ≈ [0.999, 0.0005, 0.0005]. At T = 5, logits become [2, 0.2, 0.2] and π ≈ [0.55, 0.22, 0.22] — same predicted class, softer probabilities.
items.explanation: Temperature rescales logits before softmax, useful when you need calibrated probabilities rather than only the argmax label.
